pkg/statelessx.purpose: Adapter utilities for the stateless state machine library — provides type-safe action and condition wrappers that integrate with models.Validator so state machine transitions can validate their parameters before executing.

patterns.name: WithParameters wraps typed handlers
patterns.description: Use WithParameters[T models.Validator] to adapt a func(ctx, T) error into the stateless ...any signature; it validates the argument with T.Validate() before calling the inner function.

patterns.name: AllOf joins action errors without short-circuit
patterns.description: AllOf(fn1, fn2, ...) runs all ActionFn callbacks even if earlier ones fail, then returns errors.Join of all failures. Use when multiple side effects must all be attempted.

patterns.name: BoolFn and Not adapt plain booleans to stateless guard signatures
patterns.description: BoolFn(fn func() bool) produces a func(context.Context, ...any) bool accepted by stateless guards; Not inverts a plain bool func.

key_file_guides.role: Defines ActionFn type alias, AllOf combinator, and WithParameters generic adapter
key_file_guides.watch_for: WithParameters expects args[0] to implement models.Validator; passing a non-Validator type panics at runtime with a type assertion failure

key_file_guides.role: Provides BoolFn and Not helpers for stateless guard functions
key_file_guides.watch_for: BoolFn discards both ctx and args — not suitable for guards that need per-trigger parameter access

anti_patterns: Calling models.Validator.Validate() manually before passing to WithParameters — WithParameters already calls it
Using stateless ...any directly instead of WithParameters when the trigger carries a typed argument — loses compile-time safety

decisions.decision: WithParameters constrains T to models.Validator rather than any
decisions.rationale: Forces callers to define Validate() on input types, catching structural errors at the boundary before state transitions commit any side effects

code_examples.scenario: Register a state machine transition with a validated typed parameter

pkg/strcase.purpose: Minimal string-case conversion utilities (snake_case ↔ camelCase) with no external dependencies, used wherever JSON field names or database column identifiers need to be converted to/from Go naming conventions.

patterns.name: Only underscore is treated as word separator
patterns.description: SnakeToCamel only splits on '_'; hyphens and slashes pass through unchanged. CamelToSnake only splits on uppercase runes.

patterns.name: Round-trip guarantee for well-formed identifiers
patterns.description: CamelToSnake(SnakeToCamel(x)) == x and SnakeToCamel(CamelToSnake(x)) == x for identifiers that contain only letters, digits, underscores, and no consecutive underscores.

key_file_guides.role: Two pure functions: SnakeToCamel and CamelToSnake
key_file_guides.watch_for: First character is not uppercased by SnakeToCamel — result starts lowercase; CamelToSnake does not insert underscore before the first character even if it is uppercase

anti_patterns: Using this package for PascalCase conversion — SnakeToCamel preserves the first character's case
Expecting kebab-case or dot-notation to be handled — only underscore delimiters are understood

decisions.rationale: Avoids pulling in a heavy strcase library for two simple transformations used in generated or utility code

pkg/timeutil.purpose: Shared time-interval primitives (ClosedPeriod, OpenPeriod, StartBoundedPeriod, Recurrence, Timeline) used throughout billing, subscription, entitlement, and metering to reason about billable windows, recurrence schedules, and event timelines with explicit boundary semantics.

patterns.name: Three Contains variants with documented boundary semantics
patterns.description: Every period type implements the Period interface: Contains (inclusive start, exclusive end), ContainsInclusive (both inclusive), ContainsExclusive (both exclusive). Always pick the variant matching the billing/entitlement spec — mixing them silently changes charge window edges.
patterns.example: period.Contains(t) // [from, to) — default billing window check

patterns.name: Recurrence is anchor-based, not start-based
patterns.description: Recurrence.Anchor can be any point in time past or future; iteration walks forward/backward from the anchor. Use NewRecurrence or NewRecurrenceFromISODuration constructors — they call Validate().

patterns.name: Boundary enum controls inclusive/exclusive edge of iteration
patterns.description: IterateFromNextAfter and IterateFromPrevBefore accept a Boundary argument. Inclusive returns t itself if it falls on a boundary; Exclusive skips to next/prev.

patterns.name: OpenPeriod uses *time.Time to model half-open and unbounded intervals
patterns.description: nil From means open start; nil To means open end. Operations (Intersection, Difference, Union, IsSupersetOf) handle all nil combinations. Touching periods (sharing an endpoint) do NOT overlap — Intersection returns nil.
patterns.example: OpenPeriod{From: &t1, To: nil} // half-open, extends to infinity

patterns.name: Timeline auto-sorts and provides segment decomposition
patterns.description: NewTimeline sorts its input; use GetClosedPeriods() for segment-between-timestamps or GetOpenPeriods() for the fencepost open-interval view (n timestamps → n+1 open periods).

key_file_guides.role: Period interface — three Contains variants all types implement
key_file_guides.watch_for: Any new period type must implement all three methods; missing one will cause compile errors at assertion sites like var _ Period = MyPeriod{}

key_file_guides.role: Fully-bounded [from, to] interval with Overlaps, Intersection, Truncate, Equal helpers
key_file_guides.watch_for: Overlaps returns false for exactly-touching periods ([1,2],[2,3]); use OverlapsInclusive when sequential periods must be treated as adjacent

key_file_guides.role: Half or fully-open interval with Intersection, Difference, Union, IsSupersetOf
key_file_guides.watch_for: Intersection of touching open periods returns nil — this is intentional and matches billing window semantics where [t,t] has zero length

key_file_guides.role: Recurrence schedule driven by ISODuration interval and anchor; RecurrenceIterator enables bidirectional stepping
key_file_guides.watch_for: MAX_SAFE_ITERATIONS = 1_000_000 cap; fractional ISO durations cause addIntervalNTimes to return an error; backward iteration past 1733 is explicitly not supported per TODO comment

key_file_guides.role: Generic sorted sequence of Timed[T] with period decomposition and Before/After filters
key_file_guides.watch_for: GetClosedPeriods with a single point returns a zero-length period (From==To); GetOpenPeriods always returns len(times)+1 segments
Always call boundaryBehavior.Validate() before using it — Recurrence methods do this internally but callers of custom iterator logic must validate themselves

anti_patterns: Using time.Duration arithmetic instead of ISODuration for month/year recurrences — month lengths vary and Duration math gives wrong billing period ends
Calling Recurrence methods without anchoring to a truncated time — sub-millisecond anchor drift produces off-by-nanosecond period boundaries
Assuming Overlaps and OverlapsInclusive are interchangeable — sequential billing periods must use Overlaps (returns false) to avoid double-counting
Constructing OpenPeriod{From: &t, To: &t} expecting a valid point interval — Intersection treats same-start-and-end as zero-length and returns nil

decisions.decision: Contains uses half-open [from, to) semantics as the default
decisions.rationale: Half-open intervals compose without overlap for sequential billing windows: period N ends at T, period N+1 starts at T, Contains(T) is true for N+1 only

decisions.decision: Recurrence iterates from a floating anchor rather than an epoch
decisions.rationale: Subscription anchors can be any date; billing periods must align to the original subscription start regardless of when the calculation runs

code_examples.scenario: Find the billing period containing a given usage timestamp

pkg/treex.purpose: Generic typed tree data structure (Node[T], Tree[T]) with DFS traversal, cycle detection, and shallow/deep clone operations; used by subscription and product catalog domains to model hierarchical rate-card or field descriptor trees where immutable-style updates via node swapping are required.

patterns.name: Node values must be non-nil pointers
patterns.description: NewNode[T](v T) panics if v is not a non-nil pointer. T must be a pointer type (e.g. *MyStruct). This enforces identity semantics for SwapChild/SwapNode lookups which compare by pointer equality.

patterns.name: NewTree validates acyclicity at construction time
patterns.description: Call NewTree(root) to get a validated *Tree — it returns ErrGraphHasCycle if the children graph has a cycle and ErrNodeGraphInvalid for nil children. Build the full node graph first, then wrap once with NewTree.
patterns.example: tr, err := treex.NewTree(rootNode); if errors.Is(err, treex.ErrGraphHasCycle) { ... }

patterns.name: ShallowClone for immutable-style node updates
patterns.description: ShallowClone copies the node struct, copies the children slice, and reattaches existing children to the clone. Use this to 'rename' or 'update' a node value without rebuilding the subtree; then call SwapNode/SwapChild on the parent.
patterns.example: updated := &wrapper{name: "new"}; updated.node = old.node.ShallowClone(); updated.node.SetValue(updated); root.node.SwapChild(old.node, updated.node)

patterns.name: DFS returns (stop bool, err error) — stop prunes the subtree, not the whole walk
patterns.description: Returning stop=true from the DFS callback halts descent into that node's children but sibling subtrees continue. Returning a non-nil error aborts the entire walk.
patterns.example: tr.DFS(func(n *treex.Node[*T]) (bool, error) { if skip(n) { return true, nil }; return false, process(n) })

patterns.name: Self-referential node embedding pattern
patterns.description: Domain structs that participate in a tree embed a *Node[*Self] field and set it in a constructor. This is the canonical usage pattern shown in usage_example_test.go.
patterns.example:
type FieldDescriptor struct { name string; node *treex.Node[*FieldDescriptor] }
func newFD(name string) *FieldDescriptor { fd := &FieldDescriptor{name: name}; fd.node = treex.NewNode(fd); return fd }

key_file_guides.role: Node[T] struct with parent/children management; ShallowClone and DeepClone for copy semantics
key_file_guides.watch_for: SwapChild and RemoveChild use pointer equality (c == child) not value equality — always pass the exact *Node pointer; ShallowClone reattaches existing first-level children to the clone which mutates child.parent

key_file_guides.role: Tree[T] wrapper with DFS, Leafs, and SwapNode; constructs with cycle detection
key_file_guides.watch_for: SwapNode on the root replaces t.root directly without validation — the caller is responsible for ensuring new is non-nil; DFS comment warns: if you swap a node mid-walk, backtrack immediately

key_file_guides.role: Sentinel errors for nil root, cycles, invalid graph structure
key_file_guides.watch_for: Use errors.Is for matching — all three are package-level var errors

anti_patterns: Passing value types (non-pointers) to NewNode — panics immediately
Modifying node.children slice directly instead of using AddChild/RemoveChild/SwapChild — bypasses parent-pointer bookkeeping
Continuing DFS traversal after SwapNode in the callback without stopping — the callback will follow the detached old subtree

decisions.decision: Node values are constrained to pointer types enforced at runtime via reflect
decisions.rationale: Tree mutations (SwapChild, RemoveChild) rely on pointer identity comparison; value types would make sibling lookup ambiguous

decisions.decision: Cycle detection runs at NewTree time, not incrementally on AddChild
decisions.rationale: Allows building node graphs freely before sealing them into a Tree; the DFS validation is O(n) and only paid once at construction
